records.ety: [F., fr. L. oraculum, fr. orare to speak, utter, pray, fr. os, oris, mouth. See Oral.]

defs: The answer of a god, or some person reputed to be a god, to an inquiry respecting some affair or future event, as the success of an enterprise or battle.
quotes: Whatso'er she saith, for oracles must stand.

defs: The deity who was supposed to give the answer; also, the place where it was given.
quotes: The oracles are dumb; No voice or hideous hum Runs through the arched roof in words deceiving.

defs: The communications, revelations, or messages delivered by God to the prophets; also, the entire sacred Scriptures -- usually in the plural.
quotes: The first principles of the oracles of God.

defs: The sanctuary, or Most Holy place in the temple; also, the temple itself.
quotes: Siloa's brook, that flow'd Fast by the oracle of God.

defs: One who communicates an oracle{1} or divine command; an angel; a prophet.
quotes: God hath now sent his living oracle Into the world to teach his final will.

defs: Any person reputed uncommonly wise; one whose decisions are regarded as of great authority; as, a literary oracle.
quotes: The country rectors . . . thought him an oracle on points of learning.

defs: A wise pronouncement or decision considered as of great authority.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Oracled (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Oracling (?).]
